vulnerabilities.description: Session files store potentially sensitive context data in plaintext JSON files (.claude-flow/sessions/current.json). These files contain session IDs, user context, metrics, and may include sensitive information that could be exposed if the project directory is compromised or accidentally committed to version control.

Memory storage system persists user data in plaintext JSON format (.claude-flow/data/memory.json) without encryption. Sensitive information stored via memory.set() is readable by any process with filesystem access. No access controls or data encryption implemented.

File reads from multiple package.json locations using hardcoded paths without validation of file contents. While uses fs.readFileSync safely, a compromised package.json could cause issues during version parsing (lines 61-76).

No input validation on keys or values before storage. The set() function accepts any string as key/value without sanitization, could allow storing malicious data that impacts system behavior when retrieved.

Session directory permissions not explicitly set. While atomic writes prevent races, session files created with default umask could be readable by other users on shared systems depending on filesystem permissions.

recommendations: Encrypt sensitive data in session.js and memory.js using Node.js crypto module. Consider using AES-256-GCM with a key derived from a master secret stored securely.
Implement role-based access control (RBAC) for session/memory operations. Restrict read/write access based on session ownership and user identity.
Add data sanitization/validation to memory.js set() function to prevent injection of malicious data structures that could be deserialized.
Explicitly set restrictive file permissions (0600) on .claude-flow/ directory and session files to prevent unauthorized access by other users on shared systems.
Document which types of sensitive data can be stored in session context and memory. Implement rules to prevent storing API keys, tokens, or passwords in plaintext.
Add audit logging for access to session.js and memory.js operations, especially for sensitive operations like delete() and clear().
Verify that .env files are in .gitignore and not committed. Add pre-commit hook to prevent accidental secret commits.
Consider storing sensitive secrets in OS keychain/credential manager instead of plaintext JSON files.
Review package.json dependencies for known vulnerabilities by running: npm audit
